sections.title: Getting Started
sections.description: Install jQuery 4, run code after the DOM is ready, and choose the right build.

items.title: Load jQuery 4 from the official CDN
items.notes: Use the official CDN URL when you need a direct browser script tag.

items.title: Install from npm
items.notes: Use the npm package when jQuery is bundled by a build tool.

items.title: Run code after the DOM is ready
items.notes: This short ready handler is preferred over older document-ready forms.

items.title: Use the slim build only when you do not need excluded modules
items.notes: The jQuery 4 slim build excludes ajax, effects, callbacks, deferred, and queue modules. Use the full build for those features.
items.replacement: Use https://code.jquery.com/jquery-4.0.0.min.js when ajax, effects, Deferred, Callbacks, or queues are needed.

items.title: Know the supported browsers
items.syntax: jQuery 4 supports IE 11, current major browsers, and recent mobile browsers.
items.example:
// If a project must support IE 10 or older, stay on jQuery 3.x.
// If it supports jQuery 4 browsers, test with jQuery Migrate 4 during upgrade.
items.notes: jQuery 4 drops support for IE 10 and older, Edge Legacy, and older mobile browsers.
items.replacement: Use jQuery 3.x for unsupported legacy browsers.

sections.title: Classic APIs Still Useful in jQuery 4
sections.description: Popular jQuery APIs from earlier versions that still work in jQuery 4 and remain useful in everyday code.

items.title: Chain methods on the same set
items.notes: Chaining has been a core jQuery pattern for years and remains useful in jQuery 4.

items.title: Let jQuery loop over matched elements
items.notes: Most setter methods apply to every matched element, so an explicit loop is often unnecessary.

items.title: Select an item by index
items.notes: `.eq()` returns a new jQuery object containing the element at the requested zero-based index.

items.title: Select the first or last item
items.notes: These helpers are concise alternatives to index-based selection.

items.title: Check whether an element has a class
items.notes: Use `.hasClass()` when code needs to branch based on the current class state.

items.title: Read or set form values
items.notes: `.val()` is still one of the most common jQuery form helpers.

items.title: Serialize form fields
items.notes: `.serialize()` creates a URL-encoded string from successful form controls.

items.title: Clone matched elements
items.notes: Clone templates carefully. Pass event/data options only when copying existing behavior is intentional.

items.title: Wrap elements with new markup
items.notes: Wrap helpers are useful when enhancing existing markup without rebuilding the whole node tree.

items.title: Run an event handler once
items.notes: `.one()` removes the handler after the first matching event.

items.title: Trigger an event
items.notes: Use `.trigger()` when code needs to notify existing jQuery event handlers after a scripted change.

items.title: Prevent a default browser action
items.notes: This standard event method is still used often in jQuery event handlers.

sections.title: Selectors
sections.description: Find elements with CSS selectors and jQuery selector helpers.

items.title: Select elements with CSS selectors
items.notes: Most everyday jQuery selection starts with standard CSS selectors.

items.title: Limit selection to a context
items.example:
const panel = document.querySelector(".panel");
const buttons = $("button", panel);
items.notes: A context prevents accidental matches outside a known container.

items.title: Filter a matched set
items.notes: Use filtering after broad selection when the condition is easier to express in steps.

items.title: Find descendants inside each matched element
items.notes: Use `.find()` for descendant lookup inside an existing jQuery object.

sections.title: DOM Traversal
sections.description: Move from a matched element to parents, children, siblings, and nearby nodes.

items.title: Get the parent or closest ancestor
items.notes: Use `.closest()` when you need the nearest ancestor that matches a selector.

items.title: Get children
items.notes: `.children()` only returns direct children. Use `.find()` for deeper descendants.

items.title: Get siblings
items.notes: Siblings share the same parent as the current element.

sections.title: DOM Manipulation
sections.description: Read, insert, replace, and remove page content.

items.title: Read or set text and HTML
items.notes: Use `.text()` for plain text. Use `.html()` only with trusted markup.

items.title: Insert content inside an element
items.notes: `.append()` inserts at the end. `.prepend()` inserts at the start.

items.title: Insert content outside an element
items.notes: Use these methods when the new node should become a sibling.

items.title: Remove elements or clear content
items.notes: `.remove()` deletes matched nodes. `.empty()` keeps the matched nodes but removes their children.

sections.title: Attributes, Properties, Data
sections.description: Work with attributes, DOM properties, classes, and element data.

items.title: Read or set attributes
items.notes: Attributes are markup values such as `href`, `src`, `alt`, and `aria-*`.

items.title: Read or set properties
items.notes: Use `.prop()` for live DOM properties such as `checked`, `selected`, and `disabled`.

items.title: Add, remove, and toggle classes
items.notes: In jQuery 4, do not use the removed `.toggleClass(boolean)` signature. Toggle a named class explicitly.

items.title: Store and read data
items.notes: `.data()` is useful for values associated with elements during page behavior.

sections.title: CSS and Dimensions
sections.description: Set styles, read layout measurements, and use jQuery 4-safe CSS units.

items.title: Read or set CSS properties
items.notes: In jQuery 4, specify units for CSS values that need units.
items.replacement: Use strings with units, such as `"24px"`, `"2rem"`, or `"50%"`.

items.title: Read width and height
items.notes: These methods return numbers. They are useful for scripts that need element dimensions.

items.title: Read position and page offset
items.notes: `.position()` is relative to the offset parent. `.offset()` is relative to the document.

sections.title: Events
sections.description: Attach, remove, and delegate event handlers.

items.title: Attach an event handler
items.notes: `.on()` is the main event API for current jQuery code.

items.title: Use event delegation
items.notes: Delegation works well for elements added after page load.

items.title: Remove event handlers
items.notes: Named handlers are easier to remove than anonymous functions.

items.title: Review focus event assumptions
items.notes: jQuery 4 no longer shims focusin and focusout order. Test code that depends on exact focus event order.
items.replacement: Use direct focus and blur logic when ordering is important.

sections.title: Effects
sections.description: Show, hide, animate, and stop animations when the full jQuery build is used.

items.title: Show and hide elements
items.notes: Effects are not available in the slim build.
items.replacement: Use the full build or use CSS classes with native transitions.

items.title: Fade elements
items.notes: Use short durations for interface feedback.
items.replacement: Use the full build or CSS transitions.

items.title: Animate numeric CSS properties
items.notes: Set units explicitly where needed. Effects are not available in the slim build.
items.replacement: Use explicit units and the full jQuery build.

sections.title: Ajax
sections.description: Make HTTP requests, fetch JSON, post form data, and handle jQuery 4 Ajax changes.

items.title: Fetch JSON
items.notes: The slim build does not include Ajax.
items.replacement: Use the full build or the native `fetch()` API.

items.title: Post data
items.notes: Use `.done()`, `.fail()`, and `.always()` for request outcomes.

items.title: Use the full Ajax API
items.notes: `$.ajax()` gives the most control over method, headers, data type, and callbacks.

items.title: Request JSONP explicitly
items.notes: jQuery 4 no longer auto-promotes JSON requests with a callback to JSONP.
items.replacement: Set `dataType: "jsonp"` only when JSONP is truly required.

items.title: Execute downloaded scripts explicitly
items.notes: jQuery 4 does not auto-execute Ajax script responses unless script handling is explicit.

sections.title: Utilities and Miscellaneous
sections.description: Use small helper methods that remain useful in jQuery 4.

items.title: Loop over matched elements
items.notes: Inside `.each()`, `this` is the current DOM element.

items.title: Build an array from elements
items.notes: Call `.get()` to turn the jQuery result into a plain array.

items.title: Merge objects
items.notes: Use an empty object as the first argument when you do not want to mutate defaults.

items.title: Sort and remove duplicate DOM elements
items.notes: `$.unique` was removed in jQuery 4. Use `$.uniqueSort()` instead.

sections.title: jQuery 4 Notes
sections.description: Important jQuery 4 behavior changes that beginners are likely to meet during upgrades.

items.title: Use jQuery Migrate while upgrading
items.syntax: Add jQuery Migrate 4 during testing, then remove it before final release.
items.notes: Migrate helps find removed or changed APIs during a jQuery 3 to 4 upgrade.
items.replacement: Fix warnings, then test again without Migrate.

items.title: Check jQuery UI compatibility
items.example: // Projects using jQuery UI should use jQuery UI 1.13.3 or newer with jQuery 4.
items.notes: Older jQuery UI versions should be tested and upgraded before moving to jQuery 4.
items.replacement: Upgrade jQuery UI to 1.13.3 or newer.

items.title: Use the factory entry point without a DOM window
items.notes: In Node-like environments without a global DOM window, jQuery 4 uses the dedicated factory entry point.

items.title: Trusted Types support
items.example:
// With a Trusted Types policy, pass TrustedHTML into jQuery manipulation methods.
$(".target").html(trustedHtml);
items.notes: jQuery 4 adds Trusted Types support for manipulation methods.

sections.title: Removed APIs and Replacements
sections.description: Deprecated APIs removed from jQuery 4 and the preferred replacements.

items.title: jQuery.trim
items.notes: `$.trim()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.parseJSON
items.notes: `$.parseJSON()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.isArray
items.notes: `$.isArray()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.isFunction
items.notes: `$.isFunction()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.type
items.notes: `$.type()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.now
items.notes: `$.now()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.isNumeric
items.notes: `$.isNumeric()` was removed in jQuery 4.

items.title: jQuery.nodeName
items.notes: `$.nodeName()` was removed in jQuery 4.
